last_digest.title: LA Metro Cyberattack Linked to Iranian State-Sponsored Hackers
last_digest.rss_summary:
The attack was claimed by a hacktivist group, but evidence showed it used infrastructure linked to Iranian government threat actors.
The post LA Metro Cyberattack Linked to Iranian State-Sponsored Hackers appeared first on SecurityWeek.

last_digest.title: CrowdStrike disrupts Glassworm botnet that preyed on open-source supply chain
last_digest.rss_summary:
CrowdStrike has dismantled the Glassworm botnet in an operation aided by Google and Shadowserver, stripping the operators’ access to infrastructure that helped threat actors infect hundreds of pieces of open-source software with malware since early 2025, the company said Tuesday.  The coordinated effort involved the simultaneous takedown of four attacker-controlled servers that were designed to […]
The post CrowdStrike disrupts Glassworm botnet that preyed on open-source supply chain appeared fi

last_digest.title: GPU mining malware spreads via SEO poisoning, AI chatbots
last_digest.rss_summary: Threat actors are targeting systems with high-performance computers in an ongoing cryptojacking campaign spread through a coordinated SEO poisoning operation that also manipulated AI chatbot recommendations. [...]

last_digest.title: The LA Metro Attack Wasn’t Hacktivism. It Was a State Operation With a Costume On.
last_digest.rss_summary: Iran’s “hacktivist” group Ababil of Minab, which hit LA Metro and wiped terabytes of data, is forensically linked to Iran’s intelligence service MOIS. In late March, a group calling itself Ababil of Minab posted videos and screenshots online claiming it had broken into the Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority, wiped hundreds of terabytes of […]

last_digest.title: FBI warns extortion hackers are visiting US law firms to steal data
last_digest.rss_summary: In a public advisory issued Tuesday the FBI said a hacking group has targeted law firms using social engineering schemes to gain remote access to corporate systems and exfiltrate data.

last_week_digest.title: 7-Eleven confirms data breach claimed by the ShinyHunters gang
last_week_digest.tldr: Convenience store chain giant 7-Eleven confirmed that its systems were breached in a cyberattack claimed by the ShinyHunters extortion group last month. [...]

last_week_digest.title: Microsoft disrupts cybercrime service that abused software verification systems en masse
last_week_digest.tldr: Microsoft has dismantled a cybercrime operation that generated over 1,000 fraudulent code-signing certificates, enabling threat actors to bypass security controls by masquerading malware as legitimate software. By charging up to $9,500 per certificate, the group facilitated ransomware attacks across global sectors including finance and healthcare. This disruption highlights a dangerous industry shift where attackers now exploit the foundational trust systems that organisations rely upon to verify digital authenticity.

last_week_digest.title: New Shai-Hulud malware wave compromises 600 npm packages
last_week_digest.tldr: The Shai-Hulud malware campaign recently compromised 639 versions across 323 npm packages, including popular libraries like @antv/g2. By hijacking maintainer accounts, attackers exfiltrated sensitive CI/CD and cloud credentials, creating over 2,900 rogue GitHub repositories. This incident highlights the critical vulnerability of dormant software dependencies, signalling a shift towards sophisticated supply-chain attacks that bypass traditional network security by leveraging encrypted peer-to-peer protocols to exfiltrate stolen corporate data.

last_week_digest.title: Verizon DBIR 2026: Vulnerability Exploitation Overtakes Credential Theft as Top Breach Vector
last_week_digest.tldr: The 2026 Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report reveals that vulnerability exploitation has officially surpassed credential theft as the primary vector for global cyberattacks. Driven by AI-accelerated threats and persistent patching delays, this shift forces organisations to rethink their defensive priorities. As ransomware and third-party compromises surge, businesses must recognise that traditional identity-focused security is no longer sufficient, signalling a critical need for more proactive, rapid vulnerability management strategies.

last_week_digest.title: Huawei zero-day attack behind last year’s crash of Luxembourg's entire telecoms network
last_week_digest.tldr: A sophisticated zero-day vulnerability in Huawei enterprise routers caused a nationwide telecommunications outage in Luxembourg, disabling critical mobile, landline, and emergency services for over three hours. The incident, triggered by malformed network traffic, highlights the systemic risks posed by undocumented software flaws in core infrastructure. This event signals a growing industry challenge where passive transit traffic can inadvertently crash national networks, necessitating greater transparency regarding vendor hardware vulnerabilities.

last_week_digest.title: GitHub says internal repositories were impacted in poisoned VS Code extension attack
last_week_digest.tldr: GitHub confirmed that a compromised Visual Studio Code extension led to the exfiltration of internal repositories, with approximately 3,800 repositories impacted. The incident, involving the Nx Console tool, potentially affected over 6,000 installations, forcing urgent credential rotations. This breach highlights the systemic fragility of global software supply chains, demonstrating how malicious third-party extensions can bypass traditional defences to gain deep, privileged access to critical corporate development environments.

last_week_digest.title: Microsoft warns of new Defender zero-days exploited in attacks
last_week_digest.tldr: Microsoft has patched two zero-day vulnerabilities in its Defender security suite, which threat actors actively exploited to gain SYSTEM-level privileges and trigger denial-of-service states on Windows systems. These flaws, now listed in CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, highlight critical risks to enterprise infrastructure. This incident signals an urgent industry shift, demonstrating that even foundational security software remains a high-value target for sophisticated attackers seeking widespread network disruption.

last_week_digest.title: Ukraine identifies infostealer operator tied to 28,000 stolen accounts
last_week_digest.tldr: Ukrainian cyberpolice have identified an 18-year-old operator behind an infostealer campaign that compromised 28,000 user accounts. The breach facilitated unauthorized purchases totalling $721,000, resulting in $250,000 in direct losses for the targeted online retailer. This incident highlights the growing threat of session-stealing malware, which effectively bypasses multi-factor authentication, signalling an urgent need for businesses to enhance endpoint security and monitor for anomalous session token usage globally.

last_week_digest.title: Ukraine probes teen suspect in cyber theft scheme targeting California online shoppers
last_week_digest.tldr: Ukrainian authorities have identified an eighteen-year-old suspect linked to a cybercrime operation that compromised nearly 30,000 customer accounts. Using info-stealing malware, the group executed unauthorized purchases across 5,800 accounts, resulting in $721,000 in fraudulent transactions and $250,000 in direct business losses. This incident highlights the growing sophistication of international cyber-criminal syndicates and underscores the critical need for robust cross-border law enforcement cooperation to protect global e-commerce platforms from credential-harvesting threats.

last_week_digest.title: Supply Chain Security Crisis: Too Many Vulnerabilities, Too Little Visibility
last_week_digest.tldr: The rapid acceleration of software vulnerabilities, coupled with shrinking time-to-exploitation windows, has created a critical supply chain security crisis for global enterprises. Lacking sufficient visibility into complex digital ecosystems, organisations face heightened operational risks as attackers exploit hidden weaknesses faster than patches can be deployed. This trend signals an urgent industry-wide shift toward prioritising transparency and robust risk management to prevent systemic failures across interconnected global business infrastructures.

last_week_digest.title: Chinese hackers target telcos with new Linux, Windows malware
last_week_digest.tldr: The Calypso threat group has targeted telecommunications providers across the Asia-Pacific and Middle East since 2022, deploying sophisticated Showboat and JFMBackdoor malware. By compromising Linux and Windows infrastructure, attackers established persistent footholds to facilitate long-term espionage and network pivoting. This campaign signals a growing trend of state-aligned actors sharing modular, stealthy toolkits, forcing global enterprises to prioritise advanced detection capabilities and robust cross-platform security monitoring against persistent threats.

last_week_digest.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
last_week_digest.tldr: Canadian authorities arrested Jacob Butler, the alleged operator of the Kimwolf botnet, following a cross-border investigation. The botnet compromised millions of IoT devices, facilitating record-breaking 30 Terabits per second DDoS attacks that caused individual victim losses exceeding one million dollars. This arrest highlights the growing threat of weaponised IoT infrastructure and signals a shift in global law enforcement efforts to dismantle the criminal ecosystem powering large-scale digital extortion.

last_week_digest.title: Europe dismantles VPN service used by cybercriminals to hide ransomware attacks
last_week_digest.tldr: European law enforcement agencies have dismantled First VPN, a service widely utilised by cybercriminals to facilitate ransomware attacks and large-scale fraud. By seizing 33 servers and obtaining a comprehensive user database, authorities have compromised the anonymity of thousands of criminals globally. This operation signals a shift in investigative capabilities, demonstrating that even platforms marketed as secure, jurisdictional-free havens are increasingly vulnerable to coordinated international police intervention.

last_week_digest.title: Microsoft Defender vulnerabilities exploited in the wild (CVE-2026-41091, CVE-2026-45498)
last_week_digest.tldr: Active exploitation of two Microsoft Defender vulnerabilities, CVE-2026-41091 and CVE-2026-45498, has forced urgent security updates across global enterprise environments. These flaws allow attackers to escalate privileges to SYSTEM level or disable critical antimalware protections entirely. This incident highlights the growing risks posed by public proof-of-concept exploits, signalling that security tools themselves are now primary targets, requiring organisations to prioritise rapid patch management to maintain essential operational integrity.

last_week_digest.title: Grafana Says Codebase and Other Data Stolen via TanStack Supply Chain Attack
last_week_digest.tldr: Grafana recently suffered a security breach after attackers exploited a compromised TanStack supply chain token that remained unrotated, granting unauthorised access to internal GitHub repositories. This incident resulted in the theft of proprietary codebase data, highlighting the severe operational risks posed by third-party dependencies. The event signals a critical industry shift, underscoring that even robust organisations remain vulnerable to supply chain vulnerabilities if credential hygiene protocols fail.

last_week_digest.title: Ghostwriter Is Back, Using a Ukrainian Learning Platform as Bait to Hit Government Targets
last_week_digest.tldr: The Belarus-linked threat actor Ghostwriter is targeting Ukrainian government agencies using a sophisticated phishing campaign that leverages a trusted local learning platform as a lure. By compromising legitimate accounts to deliver Cobalt Strike payloads, the group gains persistent access to sensitive systems. This resurgence signals a shift towards exploiting regional operational tools, highlighting the urgent need for organisations globally to restrict script execution to mitigate evolving espionage threats.

last_week_digest.title: Laravel Lang packages hijacked to deploy credential-stealing malware
last_week_digest.tldr: Attackers compromised four Laravel Lang repositories by overwriting 233 to 700 GitHub tags, enabling the distribution of credential-stealing malware via Composer. This sophisticated supply chain attack allowed malicious code to harvest cloud, Git, and CI/CD secrets from developers globally. The incident signals a critical shift in threat actor tactics, demonstrating how abusing repository versioning features can bypass traditional security checks, necessitating urgent improvements in software dependency verification processes.

last_week_digest.title: Why pure extortion is replacing traditional ransomware
last_week_digest.tldr: Cybercrime groups are abandoning traditional encryption in favour of pure data extortion, prioritising stealthy exfiltration over system disruption. This shift is evidenced by the 2026 Kaspersky report, showing ransom payment rates falling to 28%. Recent breaches, including the theft of 3.65 TB of data from Instructure and 8 TB from Foxconn, demonstrate that organisations can no longer rely on backups, as the primary threat is now reputational and regulatory damage.

last_week_digest.title: Tracking Iranian APT Screening Serpens’ 2026 Espionage Campaigns
last_week_digest.tldr: The Iranian-nexus group Screening Serpens has launched coordinated espionage campaigns targeting technology professionals across the Middle East, Israel, and the United States. By deploying six sophisticated remote access Trojan variants and exploiting AppDomainManager hijacking, the group successfully compromised high-value entities. This evolution signals a shift toward more resilient, technically advanced operations, forcing global organisations to reassess their reliance on trusted software configurations against persistent state-sponsored threats.

last_week_digest.title: ‘Underminr’ Vulnerability Lets Attackers Hide Malicious Connections Behind Trusted Domains
last_week_digest.tldr: The discovery of the Underminr vulnerability exposes approximately 88 million domains to exploitation, allowing threat actors to mask malicious command-and-control traffic behind trusted infrastructure. This flaw effectively bypasses standard DNS filtering, creating significant operational risks for global organisations relying on reputation-based security controls. Consequently, the industry faces a critical shift, signalling that traditional domain-based trust models are increasingly insufficient against sophisticated evasion techniques targeting modern enterprise network defences.

last_week_digest.title: Lazarus APT unveils fileless remote access Trojan designed to evade detection
last_week_digest.tldr: The North Korea-linked Lazarus group has deployed a sophisticated, memory-only remote access trojan, enabling long-term espionage without leaving forensic filesystem traces. By utilising Windows Data Protection API and disabling security hooks, the malware evades detection across multiple global financial organisations. This shift towards surgical, fileless toolsets signals a maturing threat landscape where signature-based defences are increasingly obsolete, forcing businesses to prioritise advanced behavioural monitoring and robust incident response capabilities.

last_week_digest.title: Third-Party Cyberattack Impacts Patient Information at The Oncology Institute
last_week_digest.tldr: The Oncology Institute recently confirmed a data breach originating from a third-party software vendor, potentially compromising sensitive patient information. This incident follows a broader security failure at TriZetto, which previously exposed the records of 3.4 million individuals. Such events highlight the systemic risks posed by interconnected supply chains in global healthcare, demonstrating how a single vendor vulnerability can create widespread operational consequences and significant regulatory compliance challenges.

last_week_digest.title: 7-Eleven data breach exposes personal information of 185,000 people
last_week_digest.tldr: The ShinyHunters extortion gang recently breached 7-Eleven, exposing the personal information of 185,300 individuals, including names, dates of birth, and contact details. By targeting the company’s Salesforce environment, attackers compromised sensitive franchisee documentation. This incident highlights the growing vulnerability of corporate cloud platforms to large-scale data theft. It signals a persistent trend where global retailers face significant operational and reputational risks from sophisticated groups exploiting third-party software configurations.

last_week_digest.title: Over 5,500 GitHub Repositories Infected in ‘Megalodon’ Supply Chain Attack
last_week_digest.tldr: The Megalodon supply chain attack compromised over 5,500 GitHub repositories by injecting malicious automated commits into CI workflows to harvest sensitive credentials, tokens, and encryption keys. This breach forces organisations to confront significant operational risks, as compromised secrets facilitate lateral movement and downstream supply chain exploitation. Such incidents signal a growing industry trend where attackers leverage automated CI/CD pipelines to bypass traditional perimeter defences at scale globally.

last_week_digest.title: Anthropic to release Mythos-class models to the public
last_week_digest.tldr: Anthropic’s Mythos AI has identified 23,019 software vulnerabilities, including 6,202 high-severity flaws across critical open-source infrastructure. While expanding access to governments, the company admits current safeguards cannot prevent misuse. This capability creates significant operational strain, as maintainers struggle to patch the deluge of AI-generated reports. The industry must now prepare for an era where automated vulnerability discovery accelerates the weaponisation of flaws by malicious global actors.

last_week_digest.title: Iranian APT Targets Aviation, Software Companies With Updated Tools
last_week_digest.tldr: Iranian state-sponsored threat actor Nimbus Manticore is actively targeting global aviation and software organisations using sophisticated, updated toolsets to bypass security defences. These persistent cyber espionage campaigns threaten critical operational continuity and intellectual property integrity for international firms. This activity signals a broader trend of state actors maintaining aggressive digital operations despite geopolitical pressures, forcing global enterprises to prioritise advanced threat hunting and robust infrastructure resilience strategies.

last_week_digest.title: Charter confirms data breach after ShinyHunters extortion threat
last_week_digest.tldr: Charter Communications confirmed a data breach after the ShinyHunters extortion group claimed to have stolen 40 million customer records via a vishing attack on an employee’s account. While Charter denies sensitive data exfiltration, the incident highlights the growing risk of social engineering targeting corporate SSO and SaaS platforms like Salesforce. This trend signals that global enterprises face escalating threats from sophisticated actors exploiting cloud-based service integrations.

last_week_digest.title: The Hidden Ransomware Economy Running on Exposed Databases
last_week_digest.tldr: A five-year study reveals that 30,515 exposed databases were compromised, affecting over 215 billion records across global systems like MongoDB and MySQL. Despite attackers earning only $753,000, the operational damage remains severe as data is frequently wiped or stolen regardless of payment. This trend signals that for exposed infrastructure, compromise is no longer a probability but a certainty, highlighting an urgent need for robust, automated security configurations worldwide.

last_week_digest.title: Nimbus Manticore Expanded Attacks With AI-Assisted Malware and Fake Zoom Installers
last_week_digest.tldr: The Iran-linked threat actor Nimbus Manticore has escalated global cyber operations against aviation and software sectors across the Middle East, Europe, and Australia. By deploying AI-assisted malware like the MiniFast backdoor and sophisticated trojanized Zoom installers, the group demonstrated rapid tactical adaptation during geopolitical conflict. This shift signals a concerning industry trend where state-sponsored actors leverage generative AI to accelerate development cycles and bypass traditional security defences effectively.

last_week_digest.title: Dutch authorities arrest men suspected of providing infrastructure for Russian cyber operations
last_week_digest.tldr: Dutch authorities arrested two IT entrepreneurs for allegedly providing infrastructure to support Russian cyberattacks and disinformation campaigns, resulting in the seizure of over 800 servers. By circumventing European sanctions, these suspects facilitated destabilising activities against government agencies. This operation highlights the critical role of hosting providers in geopolitical conflict and signals that regulators are increasingly targeting the technical intermediaries that enable state-sponsored digital interference and malicious operations.

last_week_digest.title: LA Metro Cyberattack Linked to Iranian State-Sponsored Hackers
last_week_digest.tldr: Iranian state-sponsored actors have been linked to a significant cyberattack against the Los Angeles Metro, exposing critical transport infrastructure vulnerabilities. While initially attributed to hacktivists, the involvement of sophisticated nation-state infrastructure highlights the escalating risk to public transit systems globally. This incident signals a dangerous shift where essential municipal services face targeted disruption, forcing international organisations to re-evaluate their operational resilience against increasingly coordinated and politically motivated digital threats.

last_week_digest.title: CrowdStrike disrupts Glassworm botnet that preyed on open-source supply chain
last_week_digest.tldr: CrowdStrike, alongside Google and Shadowserver, dismantled the Glassworm botnet, which compromised over 300 GitHub repositories and numerous open-source packages since early 2025. By targeting developer workflows, the attackers threatened global software supply chains across Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. This operation signals a shift toward proactive infrastructure disruption, forcing adversaries to incur significant costs and proving that collaborative, non-judicial interventions are essential for securing modern digital ecosystems.

last_week_digest.title: GPU mining malware spreads via SEO poisoning, AI chatbots
last_week_digest.tldr: Threat actors are exploiting SEO poisoning and AI chatbot recommendations to distribute GPU mining malware, specifically targeting high-performance computing systems. By bundling malicious DLLs with legitimate utility software, attackers gain persistent remote access via ScreenConnect to facilitate cryptojacking. This campaign signals a shift towards high-yield monetization strategies, highlighting how AI-generated search results can be weaponised to bypass traditional security awareness and compromise enterprise-grade hardware infrastructure globally.

last_week_digest.title: The LA Metro Attack Wasn’t Hacktivism. It Was a State Operation With a Costume On.
last_week_digest.tldr: The Iranian-linked group Ababil of Minab recently compromised the Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority, wiping hundreds of terabytes of data and disrupting internal operations for weeks. Forensic evidence confirms this was a state-sponsored intelligence operation disguised as hacktivism. This incident signals a growing trend where nation-states utilise consumer AI tools and deceptive personas to conduct destructive cyber warfare against critical infrastructure providers on a global scale.

last_week_digest.title: FBI warns extortion hackers are visiting US law firms to steal data
last_week_digest.tldr: The Silent Ransom Group is actively targeting law firms through sophisticated social engineering, including phishing and rare in-person office visits to exfiltrate sensitive data. By masquerading as IT support to gain remote access, these attackers bypass traditional defences, threatening to leak confidential client information for extortion. This shift towards physical and social manipulation signals a growing industry challenge where attackers exploit legitimate tools to evade detection.
